ideas.Univocity of being:
The **univocity of being** is John Duns Scotus's most important and influential metaphysical doctrine — his subtle and controversial claim that the concept of "**being**" (*ens*) is applied in the *same sense* (univocally) to **God** and to **creatures**, rather than in merely analogous or equivocal senses. This thesis, which marked a sharp break from the prevailing view of Aquinas, had profound consequences for metaphysics and theology, and it remains a subject of intense scholarly debate.

The issue concerns how our concepts and language can apply to God, who infinitely transcends creatures. **Aquinas** had held that terms like "being," "good," and "wise" apply to God and creatures **analogically** — not in exactly the same sense (univocally), which would demean God by making Him one being among others, nor in wholly different senses (equivocally), which would make talk of God meaningless — but by a proportional analogy that preserves both the likeness and the vast difference between Creator and creature. Scotus argued that this analogical account is unstable and ultimately fails to secure genuine knowledge of God: unless there is *some* concept common to God and creatures, meaning the very same thing in both cases, our reasoning from creatures to God would fall into equivocation, and natural theology would be impossible.

Scotus therefore maintained that there must be at least one concept — **being** itself, taken in its most abstract, indeterminate sense — that is genuinely **univocal**, predicated in the identical sense of God and creatures, of the infinite and the finite, of substance and accident. God and creatures differ not in the *concept* of being that applies to both, but in their *modes* of being — God being **infinite** being, creatures **finite** being. This doctrine grounded the possibility of a rigorous natural theology and reshaped metaphysics, though later critics (and, much later, thinkers like Heidegger and Deleuze, who took a keen interest in it) saw in the univocity of being a pivotal and consequential turn in the history of Western thought.
ideas.Haecceity — the 'thisness' that individu:
**Haecceity** (*haecceitas*, literally "**thisness**") is John Duns Scotus's famous and original solution to the medieval **problem of individuation** — the question of what makes an individual thing the unique, particular individual it is, distinct from all others of the same kind. Scotus coined the term to name the special principle that individuates each thing, and it stands as one of his most distinctive contributions to metaphysics.

The problem is this: if two individuals share the same **species** or common nature (two human beings both possess "humanity," two grains of sand the same "sandness"), what accounts for their being *distinct individuals* rather than one and the same? What makes *this* particular human *this* one and not *that* one? The dominant answer, associated with Aristotle and Aquinas, had been that **matter** individuates — that things of the same form are distinguished by being made of different parcels of matter. Scotus found this unsatisfactory (it struggles, for instance, with immaterial beings like angels, and with explaining the individuality of matter itself).

Scotus's alternative is the **haecceity**: a positive, primitive, individuating principle — a unique "thisness" — that is added to the common nature (the *quiddity* or "whatness") of a thing and contracts it to *this* singular individual. The common nature ("humanity") is, of itself, indifferent to being universal or particular; the haecceity is the ultimate reality that makes it *this* very individual, incapable of being shared or repeated. It is not a further *quality* or property (which could in principle be common), but a unique, non-qualitative individuating difference proper to each thing alone. Every individual thus has its own irreducible "thisness" that constitutes its ultimate singularity. This elegant and influential doctrine gave singular individuals a positive metaphysical ground for their uniqueness, and it has continued to attract philosophical interest down to the present day.
ideas.Primacy of the will over the intellect:
The **primacy of the will over the intellect** (a form of theological and psychological **voluntarism**) is a central and characteristic theme of John Duns Scotus's thought — his emphasis on the **will** as the higher and freer faculty, in contrast to the more **intellectualist** tradition of Thomas Aquinas, who tended to give priority to the intellect. This difference over the relative standing of will and intellect was one of the great dividing lines between the Scotist (Franciscan) and Thomist (Dominican) schools.

For **Aquinas**, the intellect held a certain primacy: the will is a "rational appetite" that necessarily follows the intellect's judgment of what is good — we will something *because* the intellect presents it as good, so that, in a sense, knowing guides willing, and the beatific vision (the ultimate happiness) consists primarily in the intellect's contemplation of God. Scotus reversed this emphasis. He argued that the **will** is the nobler and more perfect faculty, and, crucially, that it is genuinely **free** and *self-determining* in a way the intellect is not. The intellect, Scotus held, is in a sense a "natural" faculty, determined by its object (it must assent to what is evident); but the will is the seat of true **freedom** and contingency — it is not necessitated even by the intellect's judgment, and can freely choose or refrain.

This voluntarism has wide implications in Scotus's philosophy. It underlies his conception of human **freedom** and moral responsibility, rooting them in the self-determining power of the will rather than in intellectual necessity. Applied to **God**, it means that the divine will is supremely free — many features of the created and moral order depend on God's free choice rather than on rational necessity — and that **love** (an act of will) is higher than knowledge, so that beatitude and the union with God consist ultimately in *loving* God. This exaltation of will, freedom, and love profoundly influenced later medieval and modern thought about liberty and the self.

works.Ordinatio:
The *Ordinatio* is John Duns Scotus's major work and his philosophical and theological masterpiece — the carefully revised and authoritative version of his lectures (a commentary, in the standard medieval form, on the *Sentences* of Peter Lombard) that Scotus himself prepared and "ordered" (hence *Ordinatio*) for a permanent, definitive statement of his thought. It is the fullest and most reliable source for his mature philosophy, and one of the most intellectually demanding works of the entire scholastic tradition, earning Scotus his honorific title, the "**Subtle Doctor**" (*Doctor Subtilis*).

Like other *Sentences* commentaries, the *Ordinatio* is organized as a vast sequence of **questions** arising from the topics of Lombard's textbook — God, creation, the Trinity, the incarnation, ethics, the sacraments, and more — but Scotus uses this framework to develop, with extraordinary rigor, subtlety, and originality, his own distinctive positions across metaphysics, epistemology, philosophy of religion, and ethics.

It is in the *Ordinatio* that Scotus works out his most famous and influential doctrines: the **univocity of being** (that "being" is predicated in the same sense of God and creatures, securing the possibility of natural theology); his rigorous reformulations of the **arguments for God's existence** and the divine attributes; his theory of **individuation by haecceity** (the unique "thisness" that makes each thing the individual it is); the **formal distinction** (a subtle intermediate between a real and a merely conceptual distinction); and his **voluntarism**, emphasizing the primacy and freedom of the will. The reasoning throughout is famously intricate and precise, densely argued and rich in fine distinctions. Difficult but profound, the *Ordinatio* established Scotus as one of the greatest of the medieval philosophers and the founder of the Scotist school, whose influence extended through the later Middle Ages and beyond.
works.Quodlibetal Questions:
The *Quodlibetal Questions* of John Duns Scotus are a collection of his responses to questions arising from the special public academic disputation known as the *quodlibet* — a distinctive and demanding exercise of the medieval university in which a master would undertake to answer questions on *any* topic whatsoever (*de quolibet*, "about anything"), posed freely by any member of the audience. Held solemnly around Advent or Lent, these were the most prestigious and challenging of scholastic disputations, and a master's *quodlibeta* often represent his thought at its most mature and flexible.

Scotus's single set of *Quodlibetal Questions*, dating from his time as a master of theology (probably at Paris), is regarded as among his most polished and important works — some scholars consider it, alongside the *Ordinatio*, one of the best sources for his considered views, precisely because the quodlibetal format required him to address specific problems directly and to defend his positions against live objections. The questions range across the central topics of metaphysics and theology, treating such matters as the divine nature and attributes, the relations within the Trinity, the metaphysics of relation, the human soul and its knowledge, and questions of being and existence.

As with his other writings, the *Quodlibetal Questions* display the qualities that earned Scotus the title "the Subtle Doctor": rigorous logical analysis, fine and original distinctions, and characteristic doctrines such as the univocity of being and his subtle account of the divisions and distinctions among things. Because the *quodlibet* demanded engagement with whatever difficulties the audience raised, the work offers a valuable view of Scotus grappling flexibly and precisely with a wide array of problems, and it remains an essential source for understanding the full range and depth of his influential and challenging philosophy.
